description: Terminology glossary for consistent translations across all languages

terms.definition: Biblical Hebrew term meaning 'those who came from the sky'. Refers to the extraterrestrial civilization that created life on Earth.
terms.notes: Always capitalize. Do not translate as 'God' or 'gods'.

terms.definition: President of the Council of the Eternals on the Elohim planet. Led the scientific team that created life on Earth.
The new religious movement founded by Claude Vorilhon (Raël) based on messages from the Elohim.

translations.ja: ラエリアン・ムーブメント

terms.definition: Born Claude Vorilhon, the last prophet who received messages from the Elohim.
The current precessional age (1945-4105 CE) characterized by revelation and enlightenment.
The ~26,000 year precessional cycle, consisting of 12 ages of ~2,160 years each.
The slow wobble of Earth's axis that causes the equinox points to shift through the zodiac.
The hypothesis that extraterrestrial beings visited Earth in antiquity and influenced human civilizations.
The hypothesis that life was created by an intelligent agent rather than random processes.

translations.ja: インテリジェント・デザイン

terms.definition: The first book of the Bible, interpreted as an account of the Elohim's creation activities.
The name of this knowledge base, referencing Ezekiel's vision.
The governing body of the Elohim on their home planet, led by Yahweh. Makes decisions about the creation, custody, and ongoing relationship with humanity.
The canonical Jewish scriptures, comprising the Torah, Prophets, and Writings. Also known as the Tanakh.
The academic discipline of comparing myths across cultures to identify shared structures, motifs, and possible common origins.
The scientific field that designs and constructs new biological systems, including the de novo synthesis of organisms — the modern analogue of what the Elohim are described as having done on Earth.
The belt of constellations along the ecliptic through which the Sun, Moon, and planets appear to move. The precessional age framework uses the astronomical zodiac, not the astrological sign system.
terms.notes: Astronomical sense (the band of constellations) — distinct from astrological sign-set. In German prefer Tierkreis (astronomical) over Sternzeichen (astrological).

terms.definition: In the corpus's reading, the Hebrew *ha-satan* — 'the accuser' or 'the tester' — designates a faction in the divine council that argues against the confident plans of others. The corpus uses the Hebrew sense, not the later Christian-demonological one.
terms.notes: ru uses transliterated Hebrew '*ха-сатан*' (italicised) per 2026-06 project decision — preserves the philological argument the corpus is making about the Hebrew root, vs. conventional 'Сатана' which carries the Christian-demonological sense. zh/zh-Hant use Union Version (和合本) Protestant convention 撒但. he rendering: שָׂטָן (native form, with niqqud).

terms.definition: The first major section of 1 Enoch (chapters 1–36), recounting the descent of the Watchers — a class of divine beings — to Earth, their hybrid offspring (the Nephilim), and the divine response. A primary apocryphal source for the corpus's reading of pre-flood events.
terms.notes: The class of beings themselves: 'Watchers' / 'Veilleurs' / 'Стражи' / 'Wächter' etc. — the same translations apply with the 'Book of' prefix dropped. he: ספר הצופים (alternative: ספר העירים).

terms.definition: The Aboriginal Australian conception of the foundational creative period — neither past nor present in linear time but a continuously-active substratum of reality. Surfaces in the corpus as one of the cross-cultural traditions preserving the creation-narrative shape.
terms.notes: Often rendered as 'the Dreaming' with definite article. de 'Traumzeit' is the standard German anthropological term. ja uses katakana loanword; alternatives include ドリームタイム. he: הזמן החלום.

terms.definition: The Greek philosophical-tragic term for overweening pride that invites divine correction. The corpus uses it as a moral category recurring across traditions — most explicitly in the Crichton/Malcolm warning in the Preamble, and structurally in the Babel and Eden narratives.
terms.notes: Often italicised in body prose as a Greek loanword. fr keeps the Greek form 'hubris' (italicised) per French scholarly convention. de 'Hybris' is the naturalised German form (capitalised as all German nouns). zh/zh-Hant 傲慢 conveys the moral sense but loses the Greek-tragic connotation; consider keeping 'hubris' transliterated in Latin script alongside on first mention. ru 'хюбрис' is one of several competing transliterations (also: гюбрис); verify with native-speaker reviewer on first canonical use. he: הוּבְּריס (transliterated Greek with niqqud).

terms.definition: Giorgio de Santillana and Hertha von Dechend's 1969 study reconstructing precessional cosmology as a cross-cultural framework preserved in world mythology. One of the corpus's two interpretive backbones (the Raëlian source material being the other).
terms.notes: Book title — preserve in italics as a work title. zh-Hant variant '哈姆雷特之磨' is closer to literary Chinese register; '哈姆雷特的磨坊' is also acceptable. ja '臼' (mortar/mill) emphasises the cosmological grinding-stone metaphor. he: standard practice keeps the English title with a Hebrew transliteration in parens (טחנת המלט).

terms.definition: Ancient Greek poet (c. 700 BCE) whose *Theogony* and *Works and Days* preserve the five-ages scheme (Gold, Silver, Bronze, Heroic, Iron) that the corpus reads as a Greek variant of the cross-cultural age-of-the-world framework.
terms.notes: Standard scholarly transliterations across languages. Adjectival form ('Hesiodic') derives regularly in each language (de: hesiodisch; fr: hésiodique; ja: ヘシオドスの; etc.). he: הסיודוס.

categories.core: Core terminology that defines the Raelian/Elohim narrative
categories.timeline: Terms related to cosmic ages and chronology
